food_subgroup: One-pot soft rice and moong dal porridge

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Khichdi. Nearest parent is a cooked rice-lentil porridge. A new concept for Indian one-pot rice-legume porridges is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Soft cooked rice and moong dal porridge, Indian style (closest parent)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Khichdi; A03SM (cooked rice preparations) used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for Khichdi as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Khichdi is approximately 300 g (1 bowl), providing around 276 kcal, 9.6 g protein, and 52.5 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Khichdi — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, derived from white rice and moong dal composition data
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods composition data for white rice and split yellow moong dal in typical Khichdi proportions (approximately 60% rice, 30% moong dal, 10% ghee and spices by dry weight). Values represent the cooked dish at approximately 70% moisture content.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Rice and Legume Mixtures — University of Sydney GI Database, estimated from soft-cooked white rice and moong dal mixture
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 65 estimated from University of Sydney GI Database data for soft-cooked white rice (GI ~72) and split moong dal (GI ~31). The moong dal component provides protein and soluble fibre that meaningfully lowers the blended GI compared to plain rice porridge. Glycaemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 52.5) / 100 = 34.1, rounded to 34.2. Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 142 mg per 100 g; approximately 426 mg per standard 300 g bowl
hypertension.guidance: Khichdi is naturally low in sodium and can be prepared with minimal added salt; individuals with hypertension should limit added salt during cooking and avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as pickle to keep the per-meal sodium within recommended limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI of 65; glycaemic load approximately 34 per 300 g bowl
type2_diabetes.guidance: The moong dal in Khichdi provides protein and soluble fibre that moderates the glycaemic response compared to plain rice porridge; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume moderate portions (150–200 g) and pair with a non-starchy vegetable side to further reduce the glycaemic load per meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.9 g per 100 g), negligible trans fat, and cholesterol of 4 mg per 100 g from ghee
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Khichdi is a low-fat, low-cholesterol dish with a favourable macronutrient profile; the small amount of ghee contributes butyrate and fat-soluble vitamins, and the moong dal provides plant protein and fibre that support healthy lipid profiles, making it a heart-friendly staple when consumed in standard portions.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very low purine content; turmeric provides curcumin with anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Moong dal is the easiest legume to digest and has very low purine content; turmeric provides curcumin and ghee provides butyrate, making Khichdi traditionally considered the most anti-inflammatory and gut-healing Indian food and generally well-tolerated by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Khichdi is India's original comfort food and most widely prescribed therapeutic dish — a simple, soothing one-pot porridge of white rice and split yellow moong dal cooked together with water, turmeric, cumin seeds, and a small amount of ghee until the rice and dal lose their individual structure and merge into a smooth, slightly pourable, pale-yellow porridge. The texture is soft and homogeneous, the flavour is mild and earthy with a gentle warmth from turmeric and cumin, and the aroma is subtly nutty from the ghee tempering. Nutritionally, Khichdi provides a complete amino acid profile from the complementary rice-dal combination, with 92 kcal, 3.2 g protein, 1.8 g fat, and 17.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g. It is low in sodium, low in saturated fat, and easy to digest. It is the most widely recommended therapeutic food in Indian dietary practice — prescribed for convalescence, upset stomachs, fevers, infant weaning, and post-surgical recovery. The medium GI of approximately 65 is meaningfully lower than plain rice porridge due to the protein and soluble fibre contribution of moong dal. Khichdi is vegetarian, halal, and gluten-free, and is consumed across all regions of India as a daily staple, comfort meal, and healing food.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from ICMR-NIN composition data for the component ingredients (white rice and split yellow moong dal) rather than a direct laboratory analysis of the prepared dish. The proportions used are representative of standard Indian home-cooking practice. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for the component foods. Overall confidence is medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for the prepared Khichdi dish are not available in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content of the prepared dish varies with cooking water ratio and is not reported as a fixed value in the ICMR-NIN source. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN source for this preparation). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as Khichdi is a plant-based dish containing no animal-derived vitamin B12 sources (ghee contains negligible B12); this is naturally zero for this vegetarian preparation. The FoodEx2 code A03SM is used as the closest parent for cooked rice preparations; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Khichdi. GI value of 65 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for soft-cooked white rice and split moong dal; direct GI measurement of Khichdi has not been published in the University of Sydney database as of 2026.
